region_of_origin: Global (widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301103 covers tea beverage broadly; no specific node for sweetened flavoured RTD tea exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages; no specific node for sweetened flavoured packaged tea. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for sweetened flavoured packaged tea beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve bottle or can of packaged flavoured tea is approximately 500 ml (500 g), providing around 125 kcal and 42.5 g of sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured tea (packaged) — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for flavoured tea (packaged), CR ID D09020035. Values are reported per 100 g of beverage.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for generic packaged flavoured tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 56 is estimated from the closest analogous food: sweetened soft drink / sugar-sweetened beverage with similar sugar content (~8–9 g/100 g), which has a published GI of approximately 56–63 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Mid-range value of 56 used conservatively. GL per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 56 × 33.8 / 100 = 18.9 for a 500 g serving (6.76 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 5 = 33.8 g total carbohydrate per 500 g serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.5 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 13 mg/100 g but provides no offsetting benefit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage: the 8.5 g/100 g sugar content exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold, and habitual consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with elevated blood pressure and weight gain regardless of low sodium content (AHA 2018); switch to unsweetened tea.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.5 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely: it is a sugar-sweetened beverage delivering 8.5 g of rapidly absorbed sugar per 100 ml with no fibre to attenuate glycaemic response, and habitual consumption of sweetened tea beverages is significantly associated with elevated HbA1c; replace with unsweetened tea.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (8.5 g/100 g) in a liquid form promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and contributes to excess caloric intake; no beneficial nutrients (fibre, potassium, polyphenols from unsweetened tea) are present in meaningful quantities
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage: the high free sugar content (8.5 g/100 g) in liquid form is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk; the absence of fibre and beneficial micronutrients means there is no compensating cardioprotective benefit; unsweetened tea is strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar content (8.5 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines; no confirmed anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3, polyphenols from genuine tea extract, whole-food fibre) are guaranteed in this generic packaged product
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage: the 8.5 g/100 g refined sugar content promotes inflammatory mediators, and the absence of confirmed polyphenol content in a generic flavoured tea means no anti-inflammatory benefit can be assumed; unsweetened green or black tea is strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured tea (packaged, ready-to-drink) is a commercially manufactured sugar-sweetened beverage consisting primarily of water, tea extract, added sugar, and artificial or natural flavouring agents. It is sold in bottles or cans and requires no preparation. The beverage is lightly sweet with a mild tea flavour, typically fruit-infused or herbal-flavoured, and has a thin, watery texture similar to diluted juice. It is widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a convenient on-the-go drink, commonly purchased from convenience stores, vending machines, and supermarkets. Nutritionally, it provides 25 kcal per 100 ml, with 6.76 g of carbohydrate and 8.5 g of sugar per 100 ml (note: the HPB source reports sugar slightly exceeding total carbohydrate, which is a known data anomaly). Protein and fat are both zero by product formulation — this is a carbohydrate-only beverage with no meaningful protein or fat content. Dietary fibre is zero. Sodium is low at 13 mg/100 ml. A standard 500 ml bottle delivers approximately 125 kcal and 42.5 g of free sugar, making it a significant source of discretionary sugar in the diet. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high free sugar content and absence of fibre or beneficial micronutrients. Unsweetened tea is strongly preferred for all health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D09020035), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The match is exact. Confidence is high for all core macros and secondary nutrients reported by HPB. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI only) as no direct GI measurement exists for this generic product category.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero as this is a sugar-sweetened tea beverage with no protein-containing ingredients by formulation. fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo (D09020035). saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. Data anomaly: the HPB source reports sugar_g (8.5) exceeding carbohydrate_g (6.76) per 100 g, which is physically inconsistent. This anomaly is preserved as reported from the approved source; users should be aware that the sugar figure may include measurement rounding or methodological differences in the original HPB analysis. Glycemic load is calculated using the reported carbohydrate_g of 6.76 per 100 g for a 500 g serving: GL = 56 × (6.76 × 5) / 100 = 56 × 33.8 / 100 = 18.9. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this generic packaged beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D09020035. Multilingual local names are provided as this is a widely consumed commercial product in Singapore, thus falling under the Singaporean dishes rule for multilingual naming.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type to 'closest_parent' as per G5 requirements.
Added mandatory local names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to local_names.
Added these new local names to the flat 'aliases' array and the 'aliases_by_language' object to ensure consistency and meet G6 and Q8 requirements. This addresses the previous REJECT reasons.
